params.text: Scarcity: The fundamental economic problem of having seemingly unlimited human wants and needs in a world of limited resources.

narration: Scarcity is the core challenge in economics. It's about our endless desires clashing with finite resources.

params.text: Think about it: Time, money, natural resources – all are limited. But our desires for goods, services, and experiences are endless.

narration: Imagine you have limited time and money, but want to buy a new phone, go on vacation, and save for the future. You can't do everything.

params.text: Because resources are scarce, we must make choices. Every decision involves giving something up to get something else.

narration: This fundamental limitation forces individuals, businesses, and governments to make choices about how to allocate their resources.

params.text: Opportunity Cost: The value of the next best alternative that was not taken when a decision was made due to scarcity.

narration: And with every choice comes an opportunity cost – the value of what you gave up by choosing something else.

params.text: If you spend your last $10 on a coffee, the opportunity cost might be the lunch you could have bought, or the savings you could have made.

narration: For instance, if you choose to study for an exam, the opportunity cost might be the movie you missed with friends.

params.text: Scarcity is a universal problem, affecting everyone from individuals to entire nations, driving all economic decisions.

narration: Understanding scarcity and opportunity cost is key to understanding how economies function and why decisions are made.
